library offers two distinct but related objects. For calculating accrued interest, the
object, with its year, month, and day attributes, provides sufficient detail. The
object includes additional attributes such as hour, second, and microsecond, that are unnecessary in the calculation of accrued interest. The more detailed attributes of the
object will be needed  in other chapters.
object takes the following integer arguments:
✂️ Copy the code snippet that creates a datetime and date

my_datetime=datetime(2026, 3, 17)

my_date=date(2026, 3, 17)

#Display price and date and settlement
display(f'datetime {my_datetime}  Date  {my_date}')
Attributes of datetime and date objects

children.label: Attributes of datetime and date objects

children.value: Similar to other Python objects, the attributes of datetime and date attributes are accessed using dot notation.
: year, month, day, hour, minute, second, and microsecond.
💡 Quick Check: On datetime and date attributes
What is the year attribute of my_date?
What is the minute attribute of my_datetime?
What is the hour attribute of my_date?
👉 Click here to reveal the answer
date does not have an hour attribute

module, frequently used for generating monthly or yearly calendars, also serves a crucial function in calculating accrued interest by identifying the last day of a month. This is relevant for bonds that mature on the last day of a month, as all payment dates will similarly fall on the last day of a month.
the day of the week for the first day of the month (with Monday represented as zero);
the last calendar day of the month;
The code below shows the results for November 2025.

# The year is 2025 and the month is 11
first,last=calendar.monthrange(2025,11)
display(f'Day Of Week for First Day is {first} And Last Day Of The Month is {last}')

text/plain.content: 'Day Of Week for First Day is 5 And Last Day Of The Month is 30'

children.value: Determine if maturity date is last day of the month

children.label: Determine if maturity date is last day of the month

children.value: A common Pythonic convention is to use an underscore (_) as a “throwaway” variable name for values you don’t need—in this case, the index for the first day of the month. The variable
is then assigned a Boolean value by checking if
is equal to the maximum number of days in that month.

maturity_date=date(2035,4,30)
_,last=calendar.monthrange(maturity_date.year,maturity_date.month)
isLast=maturity_date.day==last
display(f'Maturity date of  {maturity_date} is last day of month? {isLast}')

text/plain.content: 'Maturity date of  2035-04-30 is last day of month? True'

children.value: 💡 ✍️ Application: Get the last day of the month
Is 29 the last day of month in February 2025.
Is 28 the las day of the month in February 2028.
👉 Click here to reveal the answer
calendar.monthrange(2025,2) 28 is the last day of the month
calendar.monthrange(2028,2) 29 is the last day of the month
calendar.monthrange(2100,2) 28 is the last day of the month
method facilitates date and time calculations. By knowing either the last or next schedled-payment date,

can be employed to deduce the other. The method accepts arguments specifying time intervals in years, months, days, and so forth. For instance, a semi-annual bond would require assigning a value of six to the
The cell below demonstrates the use of
by adding and subtracting six months from
. It’s important to note that this operation disregards the month-end, effectively adding or subtracting six months minus one day.

# Assign delta_time the value returned by relativedelta: months=6
delta_time=relativedelta(months=6)
# Add six months to the maturity_ date
display(f'Six Months After Maturity Date {maturity_date+delta_time}')
# Subtract six months from the maturity_date
display(f'Six Months Before Maturity Date {maturity_date-delta_time}')
💡 Quick Check: Add six months to June 30
Did you add a half year to the date?.
👉 Click here to reveal the answer
You add six months but not half a year. The new date is December 30
objects are suitable for handling individual or small numbers of dates. Pandas
objects are designed for managing large collections of dates simultaneously. A Pandas
but offers greatly enhanced functionality. This chapter will demonstrate how to utilize Pandas
to efficiently generate sequences of dates needed for determining the scheduled and actual bond payment dates.
Copy this code to create a
Two methods are used to create indexes starting on February 29, 2024. The examples illustrate how the choice of offset affects the index when adding months.
automatically adjust for month ends (e.g., adding one month to Feb 29 yields Mar 29).
, which ensures the date remains the last day of the month after the offset (e.g., adding one month to Feb 29 yields Mar 31).
Copy and run the code below to see the result:

import pandas as pd
from datetime import date

start = pd.Timestamp(date(2024, 2, 29))

# Standard offset - does not account for month end
index_standard = pd.DatetimeIndex(start + pd.DateOffset(months=i) for i in range(5))
# Note: The original code had a typo (display(datime_index)) which is fixed here.
# For demonstration purposes, we will display the standard index as well.

# Account for month end
index_month_end = pd.DatetimeIndex(start + pd.offsets.MonthEnd(i) for i in range(5))

print(f'Standard DateOffset: {index_standard}')
print(f'Accounted For Month End: {index_month_end}')
**Question:**If you make the range(13), does the
👉 Click here to reveal the answer
It automatically adjust for the last day of February and doesn’t return an invalid date.
